speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number four in an interview with Emanuel Mittelman. OK, when we left off, you were describing that you were in Birkenau now, and you were building something. What were you building?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were building barracks in which they housed future refugees that they brought in the new transports. And because they were bringing them in to such an extent that they needed more and more and more place. This is why they went through all the trouble to train people to erect all these barracks, but more of permanent structure, because they were done with brick. The ones before were just all out of wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we at now?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are in 1943 like towards the, I would say, fall.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when was it that you became aware that people were being gassed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1942, I would say also about fall that I-- that I-- well, you know first, things-- were going around-- there were flying different kind of rumors until such a time that some of us went through the places where the Sonderkommando 1 would do the actual gassing. Had-- had the chance to-- they were still living with us at such a time. It wasn't until a later time that they completely took them away, and they were not able to stay with the rest of us. They were quarantined. They told us about these-- about these shower heads and all that stuff. So they got a message from one to another. You have to remember than in the beginning, people had a hard time believing things like this, a little bit, until you had one guy coming back, like myself, and telling people, hey, I was covering a mass grave. Don't tell me, I know what-- what happened. And so little by little, it spread, until-- until everybody knew what's really going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as long as you had the-- the strength in you to-- to be able to walk and not to report to an infirmary, then be able to go to work. The worst thing in the world would be if you say, I'm not going to work tomorrow. So that means that's your end. So you-- you tried everything you could to get in a detail that the work wasn't so hard. Number one, try to get in a detail that wasn't Sonderkommando, because then you knew that your days are counted. And, like, we were maintaining each other, so to say, by-- by who's being more cunning. Who can get in a place where there is less hard-- hardship and so forth. Did you have a kapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We always had a kapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a little bit about their treatment to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kapos, in general I would say, to the greatest extent, in the largest majority, were all acting like these hardened criminals. The one like I mentioned, that blockalteste who was-- because they were picked for this job because of that. And once they got into the position, they knew in order for them to maintain a little higher standard-- whatever that meant-- it meant a little more food. It meant a little bit more freedom, because the kapos themselves didn't have to do any work. Their job was to hit us hard enough to see that we accomplished the task that they were given to us to do. So I would say most of the kapos used their sticks and used their-- their bats and whatever means they had to-- to abuse us to-- tremendously, let's put it this way. There were exceptions. There were exceptions that they just did it when a-- when an SS man saw it. Or they did it-- you could notice-- they did it only because they had no other choice. And they knew that if they wouldn't do it, then-- then he would do it, and he would hit us harder. But I would say, to the largest extent, they were-- they were mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you beaten personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you were-- how long were you a bricklayer, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bricklayer for approximately, I think, about until 1944 in, I think, early spring or so, at which time there was a rumor going around between the old timers-- if I can call them like this. It's like somebody being a rookie in Auschwitz. And somebody-- we're talking about now, the era where the Hungarian transports used to come in already, from Hungarian Jews. And there was a rumor going around that they're looking for guys who will work in the-- in the Canada camp. Canada, which is well known, was a name given by us to that detail. It was a special camp in Auschwitz . It was one time in Auschwitz, and then in Birkenau, where the people who worked there would, in fact, take all the suitcases that came in from newcomers, unpack them. And there were boys and girls in that camp and would do the sorting of the stuff under the SS supervision. It was a camp, that in a certain extent, was, of course, much better than the other camps. First of all, you had food galore, because in all the packages, there was all this food. And a person can only eat so much, so everybody was sharing. And I was really asked by a guy who was also from Czechoslovakia-- I forgot its name. [? Rosenwasser, ?] or whatever what his name was. He says, listen. You come to me and you will see that under my guidance-- he was-- he was the blockalteste, or the kapo, whatever, in this detail. That you will have all the food you will need, and you'll see, you will have it good. And so forth and so forth. So I came along with him to that camp. It was just a different section from Birkenau, because it was divided in-- I don't remember exactly today-- G-D-C-F and so forth. And we were the neighboring place to the crematoria. In other words, after us was already the Sonderkommando 1, where the crematorium and the gassing was going on. I mean, you could see in the night from where we were, from the chimney smoke and fire going out. And you knew, and you could smell the burning of the bodies. But this-- when we came to this place, of course, everybody who came to this place-- I don't remember how many of us were there. There were boys and girls. All of a sudden, had-- we had no problems of having warm clothing, because they did not watch us, the SS, the [INAUDIBLE] We still had to have our uniform, but under the uniform we could wear just almost anything you wanted to. And I was working in this place there under this guy's guidance, so to say, until one day he got angry at me. I don't recall for what. And he came, and he slapped me. So here, I tell myself, here is somebody who's supposed to be my buddy, my-- my-- my guy who's supposed to help me. He slapped me for some little thing that I did. I don't remember what it was. So I was very, like, hurt personally that I followed him in order to have it a little better. And now he himself is beating me up. He's a kapo. He had all the right. I mean, nobody denied him that right. So I was trying to get out of this job that I was doing right now. And I was looking into different things that I could do there to get out from under his hands, because I was like, kind of insulted, if you can understand that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, they were telling us that they were looking for a man who's in charge in the camp-- in this particular camp-- of the food. Meaning, not the food that is brought in with the packages, but every camp had a detail-- that used to go to the main camp to draw your daily rations. Like I mentioned, the coffee, the bread, the margarine, steaks, or whatever they gave you. So there was somebody in charge who had to go and-- and haul it into each camp to distribute for the people to eat. So they were looking, one day, for a guy who would-- who would be-- take this over. Nobody wanted the job, because everybody wanted to eat from the food that was in the camp that-- that came-- rather that came with the people, which was much better stuff, whichever-- whatever it was. So and I was kind of hurt by the guy what he-- that he beat me up. So I went to the guy who was in charge, whatever, and I volunteered for this job. And since my name was Mittelman, and the food that we have to draw was [GERMAN] called in German, so they called me the [GERMAN], which takes a half a word from my name and puts it in the word there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was assigned a special two rooms. One of them in which I can sleep by myself rather than with the rest of the guys in the bunk. And another room, which was like kind of a place where I keep all the stuff that I'm bringing in, the bread and so forth. Now, since this was a time were nobody was eating the food that people drew from the main canteen-- And I used to come in the morning, let's say I had-- I don't remember how many people were there, maybe 300, 400. So I would bring in, maybe, for 300, 400 people, 150 breads. And then we'd-- we'd cut them in quarters, and some margarine and some-- and some whatever that came with it. I used to go-- and then the coffee or soup or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a detail of people that had a little how shall I say? A two-wheeler, a large two-wheeler. I was in charge of it. And they-- so we used to go every morning with this-- with these few guys.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember how many there were. And we used to go to the main camp, get all this bread, bring it in here, and I used to store it away. Whoever wanted this ration would get it. But most of the guys didn't want it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what we used to do, because we were all-- because bordering the different camps is either on the way, when I-- when I came from the main camp, I would meet a detail of women or other guy-- guys who were from different camps who had no food. We'd load them up with-- with bread, and with margarine, and with-- and soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes our guys were eating, too, because they had no-- no way how to get something warm. And-- and we also had a-- a good way of becoming smugglers, you know, taking some-- some kind of valuable stuff out of the camp there and smuggle it into the main camp, and started dealing and wheeling with some other guys in the camps, you know, like bartering. You know, you gave them this for that, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things would you barter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's say we would take gold out. OK, and somebody would-- in camp, being able-- there were a lot of civilians who came in, would bring in salami, kielbasa, Polish stuff. So you would-- you would trade. You would [INAUDIBLE] because you know, everybody in Auschwitz-- most of the stuff that they were eating from these things were cans, like, you know, like maybe tuna fish. Maybe-- whatever you can put in in a can-- that's what people were-- who-- who were taking away on transports brought in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody was really starving there, but you did the diversification of food. Maybe you found some of the suitcases maybe had salami or whatever. But-- and these-- this were replenished every day, because people were coming in and out, in and out. So at-- at this point of the game, we did not have any-- I really gained weight, but now I probably-- in this-- this place, by now I probably gained all my weight that I lost through all the time. And-- and-- and-- and I wasn't sick or-- or-- or in any way. And the supervision or the beatings where almost none. So I was solely in charge of the-- all the supervision of the food. And to my own decision, I could-- not legally, of course-- give away, we were throwing-- we had fences between each camp. So when I was walking from the main camp, we were probably passing two, three different camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a gypsy camp. There was a lady-- there was a women camp. There was an infirmity camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were on the road met all kinds of people. There were all kinds of details that would go in there. And some guys were in charge of plumbing, or whatever, to empty latrines. So you used to meet them there. It was all done you know, with hand and buggy, so to say. So we'd meet these guys. You used to either barter with them, or you used to give them to they should bring in the ladies' section. And that was the first time where I had a chance-- again, my brother, the middle brother Joe, he also switched around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he became the-- I don't know what I should call this in English. You see today, the big trucks that go and clean the sewers in the street. He became the head of the department to go and keep-- keep the latrines. Because they had outhouses, really, so you had to go to the outhouses and empty them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had a detail with him, and used to go to all the camps to empty the outhouses and then carry it on someplace of the fields and empty it out. So he would create a contact with my sister who was in the women's camp. So now we already had contact between-- between the three of us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by now, my oldest brother, Meyer, he was already gone. He was in the Warsaw ghetto. They took him away to clean up after they destroyed the Warsaw ghetto. They took away a whole detail from-- from-- from Auschwitz, and my brother was-- my older brother was one of them. And we lost completely any kind of account on him. We thought that he's s gone. As a matter of fact, we had a live witness telling us that he saw him dead, but whatever. It turned out to-- to be a lie, but-- so my brother now was in contact with-- with my sister. So sometimes we used to meet on the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I used to take some bread and some stuff and give him over, and he used to schlep it in the-- in the ladies' section. So we really became you know, like a-- like a little post office that would carry messages. And so you find out how our sister feels and some others. We used to carry messages to other people who were in the camp. If somebody wanted to know, how's my sister, how's-- how's my brother. So this was like a little secret underground post office. Of course, we had to be very careful, because if they catch you, first of all, you lose your position, number one. And they probably would beat the dickens out of you. And-- and-- and if not worse, I mean. How long did that continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: That continued-- that continued till-- till the end of '44. And I have to tell you one of the incidents. Since we were now in a camp where-- where we had almost everything to dispose that we wanted to have what people bring in. So we got a hold of a-- of a Jewish prayer shawl, of a tallis. And we had-- a lot of us guys who-- you know, once you came back a little bit to strength, you also came back a little bit to your faith.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to-- when Rosh Hashanah came in 1944, we decided a bunch of us were going to make a-- a quorum, a minyan, which it's called in-- in Hebrew, and we'll conduct services. So when I was a child, I used to sing in a choir by a cantor, for which I used to get paid for. And I remembered a lot of the liturgy by heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and we had-- we had the prayer books anyway, so we organized that-- that-- that-- I don't remember how many guys were there, but 20 or 30. We got a hold of a few of these prayer shawls, and we put them on. And of course we put on guards in all the corners of the barracks. And I was conducting services there on Rosh Hashanah, 1944. And there were a lot of guys who was really mocking us because when the-- we used to say in the evening, the big fires from the crematoria-- most of the people who-- who even were believers at home lost their faith in Auschwitz. Used to come to the guys like myself who-- who-- I mean, there was nothing left in me as far as faith is, that I still said I believe in God. I hope that one day I'll be out of here. But further than that, there was no such thing as keeping Saturday or watching for kosher. It was far, far away from your mind, because you were so happy to be alive. And these guys would laugh at us when we made the-- this quorum, but a lot of them came along. So we had there about 30 or 40 people. And I was giving my cantorial rendition there with the knowledge that some guys are watching outside and that everything is safe. Except one of the guys was goofing off.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the middle of my praying one of the SS walks in with a pistol in the hand. And-- and-- and he shoots out a bullet above my head. I don't think he was aiming at me. I mean, maybe because he knew me, I don't know why, but he yelled out loud and clear, what do you think this is, a cheder, like a Jewish school? Disperse right away, or I'll kill all of you. So of course we all ran away, and we later on had to bawl that one guy out. You know, it's like putting somebody on a-- on a mission to watch. I mean, the guy could have really taken the gun and killed us all, and he would have not to report, to give an accounting for, to anybody. So at worst that I can say, that in-- in the goodness of his heart, he didn't have a-- a heart to-- to kill us. I'm sure he could have-- he could have aimed at me and shoot me, and he purposely did it above my head. I mean, I'm pretty sure at this point, because we never undertook another trial like this again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by that time, that was already getting to the end of the time where the-- where the guys who ran away from Auschwitz-- I must have read a book about this. There were a couple of guys who divulged to the world on what's going on in Auschwitz. And the pressure on the Germans got bigger and bigger. And I don't remember exactly the time, but it was about towards fall, or maybe October or so in 1944, that they did away with the crematorium. There were no more mass killings, no more gassing. They stopped that because the Germans felt a little pressure, and they were like losing the wars on all the corners, too. So they were like scared of us, so that stopped. And there were no newcomers at this point, either, so the food in camp was scarce. And I became, again, somebody, because now everybody wanted the food that I used to go every day to get. And so we lived there. I mean, we had the normal life as far as we were not undernourished. We were not overnourished. We had our ration of bread that they gave us. Nobody poured it out in the mud like in the early times, like the Russians did. So like I said, it was enough to sustain yourself. And nobody in this camp-- not the boys and not the girls-- had-- had a problem as far as sustaining themselves as far as eating. There's a-- there's another little incident I'd like to mention before I get to the end of this segment. There was a girl in this camp who apparently had a-- secretly an affair with an SS guy. I can-- I'm not going to mention names, but it-- it was divulged in Israel in all the newspapers, you know, when it came out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we don't really know whether she really had an affair with him, but we knew that they were kind of close. And he-- his name, which I would like to mention, is [INAUDIBLE]. What's his name? [INAUDIBLE]. Scharfuhrer Wunsch. Wunsch-- means in German-- I have a-- Wish.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I have a-- a wish. And he was in charge of that whole camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second in command in the Canada, second in command. And he had this tete-a-tete with that girl. And she did a lot of favors to a lot of guys because of him. In other words, she had influence on him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somebody got in-- in real bad trouble, and we knew, well, that is going to be a big case. So we went to her, and we used to ask her to intervene. And many, many times she helped out. She was a Jewish girl?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a Jewish girl, yeah. There was nobody non-Jewish in this camp, by the way. Nobody. So one day, her sister arrives with a Hungarian transport with a baby to the camp there. And she finds out somehow. I don't remember how.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were-- in this camp, we were maybe-- the end of the camp was about a hundred feet away from the place where the selection used to happen. The-- the second time, after they came back from the train in this place it was like a shower room. That's not where they gassed, but they used to, like, select. If a mother had a child, that they used to send the mother with the child to the crematorium to the gassing. If not, it-- it was like a second selection. So she finds out that-- that her sister arrived with a baby. And she runs to see her sister, and her sister begs her to do something for her. So she told her, you hold on here. And she runs to this Scharfuhrer-- Unterscharfuhrer Wunsch, and she talks to him, and she begs for her sister's life. So he went there, and he told her, listen, I'm going there. I'm going to save your sister, but I can't save the child. So you be ready to tell your sister a lie, because I'm going to separate them. In order to save her life, I have to separate them. And I remember this story as vividly as today, what happened. And this Unterscharfuhrer Wunsch went over there, took the baby from that woman, and he told her that he's going to give the baby to somebody else to hold for a while. At first she was-- you know, which mother wants to part from her baby? And she didn't know what really is going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But eventually, she stayed alive. She saved her. And they both, even today, live in Israel. And a-- a good friend of mine with whom I was in touch for many, many, many years-- and he was one of the guys who-- who I gave access to sleep in my room when I was on this detail from the [GERMAN]. His name was Maurice [NON-ENGLISH]. He's not alive anymore. He dedicated his life after the war to go to all the German trials, wherever he knew any of the Nazis or the SS by name. He would always go to testify. That was his task. He didn't-- he had incomes different, and he didn't have to get involved in the daily living.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this man, while I was visiting Israel several times, was always telling me on-- on what's going on with these different kinds of trials. So one day, when I came there for a visit, he told me that this Unterscharfuhrer Wunsch sent his wife to Israel to meet her. She should come to Germany to plead for her husband's life, because he was locked up now.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was awaiting a trial for all kinds of whatever they had against him. And she said, look, not only did my husband save your life and did you a lot of good, but he also saved your sister's life. And it's not more than fair that you should come to testify in his behalf.

speaker: interviewee
text: So these old timers, so to say-- and she was one of them, and my friend, this Maurice-- they had a-- like a group of people where they would meet every week and talk about concentration camp. That was all their discussion. It was like a club reminiscing old stuff. Had a meeting-- and they wanted in that general meeting-- they wanted to discuss what do we do? Should she go, should she not go?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- you know, like a board of directors discussing is it the right thing, or is it the wrong thing. I mean, do you have an obligation to go testify and save somebody's life, maybe, a guy who-- who-- I don't know exactly what they had against him, but if they took him to court, they must have had against him something that was serious. So they decided that she should not go. But she, on her own, decided to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my friend, Maurice, and she-- they were all paid by the German government, of course. They went there to-- he was testifying against. She was testifying in behalf. Well, he apparently got off with a-- with a small jail term. And because she was, like, pleading the court, look, he saved my sister's life, and she--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was kept a secret for many years in Israel. It wasn't until about-- I don't remember exactly-- five years, six, seven years ago when I was in Israel that a-- a daughter-- a granddaughter for the woman who was saved-- went to a magazine that is very well known in Israel. I would say like Life Magazine in here-- and divulged the whole story. It was splashed up all over. And every detail that I was just recounting here was all told there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now did-- did-- did you also have contact with this Wunsch man? This Scharfuhrer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not directly. I mean, I had contact with him that he was my, so to say, my boss. And he was the one who saw me every day going like in the main camp for-- but I was never beaten by him, or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him like perform any good deeds for anybody or help people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I-- other than, maybe, slap somebody a little, I never saw him perform any bad deeds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever seen him perform any good deeds?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing-- not other-- any other that I knew that he was doing because she intervened. I mean, somebody got into serious trouble. Remember, in camp you get in serious trouble for-- for little things. Like somebody is caught that he takes from the packages that he's supposed to sort. You find a ring in your pocket. That-- that's-- that's almost death penalty, because they-- we were all warned. You know, you take food-- you're not supposed to take food, either. And they'll catch you with a sweater because it's warm. He's not going to kill you for that, either. But to take what they're really searching for is gold, diamonds, money. That was a federal offense. I mean, that was big. And if the SS in that camp felt like pulling his gun out to shoot you, he wasn't giving any accounting to anybody for that. So it was-- so if somebody really transgressed something and was caught, and she intervened, I mean, I know by my own knowledge that he always helped out. They reduced the sentence. Whatever it was, it wasn't killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, why don't we pause here. This is the end of tape three, an interview with Manny-- Emanuel Mittelman.
